subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference
- **Standard game** — 4 to 16 players.
- **Recommended** — 8 players (two teams of 4).

#### Core Components
- **Name cards** — 440 cards total, each featuring a person, character, or thing with a name and point value (1-3).
- **Sand timer** — 1 sixty-second timer.
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

#### Card Details
- **Card face** — Shows a name/title, a brief humorous description, and a point value.
- **Point values** — 1 point (easy), 2 points (medium), 3 points (hard).

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Setup Sequence
1. **Form teams** — Divide players into two roughly equal teams.
2. **Deal cards** — Deal 8-10 cards to each player from the shuffled deck.
3. **Draft cards** — Each player secretly reviews their cards and keeps 5 they want in the game (discard the rest face-down).
4. **Build the deck** — Combine all kept cards into one shared deck and shuffle thoroughly.
5. **Prepare scoring area** — Each team needs a space to place won cards.
6. **Place timer** — Set the 60-second timer within reach.

#### Table Layout
- **Central deck** — The combined card deck sits in the center.
- **Team areas** — Each team has a pile area for scored cards.
- **Timer** — Accessible to both teams for turn management.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Team Formation
- **Two teams** — Split into two teams as evenly as possible.
- **Seating** — Teams may sit together or intermixed; it does not affect gameplay.
- **Clue-giver rotation** — Within each team, players take turns being the clue-giver each round.

#### Information Access
- **Drafted cards** — All players contributed cards to the deck, so everyone has partial knowledge of what is in the deck.
- **Shared deck** — Both teams use the same deck across all three rounds.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### Pre-First-Turn Checks
- **Deck assembled** — All drafted cards are shuffled into one deck.
- **Teams formed** — Two teams with designated first clue-givers.
- **Timer ready** — 60-second timer is available.

#### First Action
1. **Choose starting team** — Any method (coin flip, youngest player, etc.).
2. **Begin Round 1** — The first clue-giver on the starting team draws from the deck and begins describing cards using any words, sounds, or gestures.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Flow
1. **Start timer** — Flip the 60-second sand timer.
2. **Draw and clue** — Active clue-giver draws the top card and gives clues according to the current round's rules.
3. **Team guesses** — Teammates shout out guesses. No penalty for wrong guesses.
4. **Correct guess** — Place the card in the team's score pile. Draw the next card immediately.
5. **Skip option** — If stuck, place the card on the bottom of the deck and draw a new one.
6. **Timer expires** — Turn ends. Any card currently being clued goes back into the deck.
7. **Switch teams** — The other team takes their turn with a new clue-giver.

#### Round Completion
- **Deck exhausted** — When the last card is guessed, the current round ends.
- **Collect all cards** — Gather all scored cards from both teams, reshuffle them into one deck.
- **Begin next round** — Same deck, new round rules.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Round 1: Describe It (Taboo-style)
- **Any words** — Use any words, phrases, or sentences to describe the name on the card.
- **No restrictions** — Unlike Taboo, there are no banned words.
- **No gestures required** — You may gesture but words are the primary tool.
- **Cannot say the name** — You may not say any part of the name on the card.

#### Round 2: One Word Only
- **Single word** — You may say exactly one word as your clue.
- **No additional words** — After saying your one word, you must remain silent.
- **Gestures allowed** — You may use sounds and gestures to supplement your one word.
- **Cannot say the name** — You may not say any part of the name on the card.

#### Round 3: Charades Only
- **No words** — You may not speak at all.
- **No sounds** — No verbal sounds, humming, or sound effects.
- **Physical acting only** — Use gestures, body movement, and facial expressions.

#### General Rules (All Rounds)
- **Skip penalty** — Skipping costs no points but the card stays in the deck.
- **No spelling** — You may not spell out the answer.
- **Shared knowledge builds** — The same deck is used all three rounds, so descriptions from earlier rounds carry forward as shared references.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Shared Knowledge Mechanic
- **Core design** — Using the same cards across all three rounds is the central mechanic.
- **Round 1 establishes associations** — Descriptions in Round 1 create shared reference points.
- **Round 2 leverages memory** — The single word often references a Round 1 description.
- **Round 3 builds on both** — Charades reference accumulated associations from Rounds 1 and 2.

#### Card Point Values
- **Variable difficulty** — Cards are worth 1, 2, or 3 points based on obscurity.
- **Point value scoring** — Each card scores its printed point value when guessed, not a flat 1 point.

#### Team Balance
- **Uneven teams** — If teams are unequal, the smaller team still plays normally; the larger team simply has more clue-givers in rotation.
- **Clue-giver rotation** — Rotate clue-giver duty within the team each turn so everyone participates.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Scoring
- **Per card** — Each correctly guessed card scores its printed point value (1, 2, or 3).
- **Per round** — Points accumulate across all three rounds.
- **Total score** — Sum all card point values across Rounds 1, 2, and 3.

#### Game End
- **After Round 3** — The game ends when the deck is exhausted in Round 3.
- **Final tally** — Each team totals all points earned across all three rounds.
- **Winner** — Team with the highest total score wins.

#### Tiebreaker
- **No official tiebreaker** — In case of a tie, play an additional timed round or declare a shared victory.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Drafting Phase
- **Keep cards you know** — Draft cards that your team is likely to recognize.
- **Include some easy ones** — Having a few 1-point gimmes ensures Round 1 momentum.
- **High-value picks** — Keep 3-point cards if they are actable or have strong single-word associations.

#### Round 1 Priorities
- **Create memorable descriptions** — Use vivid, unusual associations that your team will remember in later rounds.
- **Establish code words** — If describing "Albert Einstein," a memorable phrase like "crazy hair genius" gives your team a handle for Rounds 2 and 3.
- **Speed over perfection** — Getting through more cards in Round 1 gives your team more shared knowledge.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Generic descriptions in Round 1** — Saying "he was a famous person" gives no useful hook for later rounds.
- **Forgetting Round 1 associations** — In Round 2, defaulting to a new association instead of referencing the Round 1 description wastes the shared knowledge advantage.
- **Skipping too often** — Every skip means the card stays in the deck; excessive skipping wastes time.
- **Not watching opponent turns** — Paying attention to the other team's clues helps if cards cycle back.
- **Rushing Round 3 without a plan** — Jumping into charades without recalling the established associations leads to flailing.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Skip or Persist
- **Persist** — Spend extra seconds if your team is close to guessing; the card's points are locked in.
- **Skip** — Move on quickly if the card is a dead end to maximize cards attempted per turn.

#### Decision 2: Round 1 Description Depth
- **Thorough** — Longer descriptions create stronger anchors but mean fewer cards per turn.
- **Brief** — Quick descriptions get through more cards but may not stick for Rounds 2 and 3.

#### Decision 3: One-Word Choice in Round 2
- **Callback word** — Reference your Round 1 description for maximum recognition.
- **New association** — Sometimes a fresh word is more evocative, but only if the original description was weak.
